title: Stripe
subtitle: Webhook-driven payments that survive retries
summary: How Credee treats Stripe as an event source of truth for money movement, fees, and recovery.
problem: User clicks are unreliable. Network timeouts, duplicate webhooks, and partial fee calculations can desync product state from what Stripe actually settled.
approach: Accept payment intent quickly, then let verified Stripe events advance durable state through idempotent workers. Never trust a single browser response as the final ledger truth.
architecture: API validates and creates local payment intent / pending records.
Stripe confirms settlement through signed webhooks.
BullMQ workers apply transitions idempotently using event IDs.
MySQL stores audit-friendly payment state for recovery and support.
lessons: Design for duplicate delivery before you design for the happy path.
Fee logic belongs in deterministic domain code, not scattered controller branches.
Support tools need the event trail as much as the final status.
outcomes: Clear pending vs settled states for clients and operators.
Safer retries when Stripe or workers hiccup under load.

title: Redis
subtitle: Latency cuts without hiding consistency problems
summary: Using Redis as a coordination and cache layer around FinTech and AI workloads without making it the source of truth.
problem: Hot paths and bursty campaign or payment traffic can overwhelm MySQL and slow every request if each call hits the database.
approach: Cache short-lived reads, rate-limit edges, and back queue coordination with Redis while keeping durable records in MySQL.
architecture: Read-through cache for hot non-critical lookups.
Queue and lock coordination for worker safety.
Explicit TTL and invalidation rules tied to write paths.
lessons: A cache that lies quietly is worse than a slow query you can see.
Separate coordination keys from business entities.
Measure hit rate and stampede risk before calling Redis a win.
outcomes: Better burst behavior for scheduling and payment side effects.

title: BullMQ
subtitle: Durable work for payments and AI jobs
summary: Turning brittle request/response paths into retryable worker pipelines for Credee and Practina.
problem: Payments, webhooks, OpenAI calls, and social publishing are too slow or too flaky to finish inside a single user request.
approach: Enqueue domain jobs with retries, backoff, and dead-letter visibility. Keep public APIs focused on accept/validate/respond.
architecture: API enqueues typed jobs after validation.
Workers own provider calls and state transitions.
Failed jobs surface with enough context to replay safely.
lessons: Idempotency keys are part of the API contract.
Queue depth is a product metric, not only an infra chart.
outcomes: 1000+ background jobs handled through production worker pipelines.
Async product behavior that can recover after provider outages.

title: OpenAI
subtitle: AI features with production boundaries
summary: Integrating LLMs into Practina and personal systems without letting model latency or cost own the request path.
problem: LLM calls are variable, expensive, and failure-prone. Shipping them as blocking request handlers creates fragile products.
approach: Shape prompts from structured product context, isolate generation behind APIs/queues, and return outputs that operators and users can evaluate.
architecture: Structured input assembly before the model call.
Response shaping for explanations, not only completions.
lessons: Cost and timeout budgets belong in the design, not the postmortem.
Explainability is a UI requirement for decision-support AI.
outcomes: AI workflows that degrade and retry instead of hanging users.
Reusable patterns across marketing and decision-support products.

title: Demand Letters
subtitle: Document workflows as production systems
summary: Building demand letter generation as a durable business process inside Credee, not a one-off export script.
problem: Legal and financial documents require consistent inputs, regenerable outputs, and a clear audit path when numbers or parties change.
approach: Model the letter as a workflow with validated data, versionable templates, and async rendering so the UI never waits on PDF assembly.
architecture: Domain validation collects parties, amounts, and contractual context.
Workers generate artifacts from canonical state.
Stored outputs remain tied to the source record for regeneration.
lessons: Document products are state machines with files attached.
Regeneration requirements should shape schema choices early.
Operators need to know which source values produced which artifact.
outcomes: Repeatable letter generation from trusted domain state.
Cleaner separation between business rules and rendering.

title: Transaction Fees
subtitle: Deterministic fee logic under real payment noise
summary: Keeping Credee fee calculations predictable when payments, refunds, and provider events arrive out of order.
problem: Dynamic fees become support incidents when calculation rules are implicit, duplicated, or recomputed differently after retries.
approach: Centralize fee rules, persist calculated snapshots with the transaction, and make recalculation explicit when inputs change.
architecture: Fee policy evaluated in domain services.
Webhook-driven adjustments produce new events instead of silent rewrites.
lessons: Money math needs fixtures and edge-case catalogs.
Never recompute historical fees from today’s policy without recording why.
Fee bugs feel like product trust bugs instantly.
outcomes: More explainable fee outcomes for users and support.
Safer evolution of fee policy over time.
